question: Under the AIM Act (40 CFR §84.54), what specific action regarding R-410A was prohibited beginning January 1, 2025?
options: The production and importation of R-410A refrigerant was banned — existing supplies may still be used for servicing equipment already in the field
The installation of any new equipment using R-410A was banned, and all existing R-410A systems must be retrofitted
The sale of R-410A was banned, including selling recovered R-410A to existing system owners
All R-410A systems must be decommissioned and recovered by January 1, 2025
answer_text: The production and importation of R-410A refrigerant was banned — existing supplies may still be used for servicing equipment already in the field
explanation: Under the AIM Act HFC phasedown rule (40 CFR §84.54), the production and importation of R-410A refrigerant was prohibited beginning January 1, 2025. This does not immediately stop all R-410A use — existing stockpiles and recovered/recycled R-410A may continue to be used for servicing R-410A systems already installed in the field. The ban targets new production to reduce HFC emissions over time. Technicians may continue to purchase and use R-410A for service work from existing inventories.
source_ref: 40 CFR §84.54; AIM Act HFC Phasedown Rule

question: After January 1, 2026, which refrigerants are prohibited in NEW high-pressure air conditioning equipment under the AIM Act GWP equipment ban (40 CFR §84)?
options: Refrigerants with GWP greater than 700, including R-410A (GWP 2088) — new equipment must use lower-GWP alternatives like R-454B (GWP 466) or R-32 (GWP 675)
All HFC refrigerants, including R-134a and R-32, regardless of GWP
Only refrigerants with GWP greater than 2,000, such as R-410A and SF6
All refrigerants except ammonia and CO2, which are the only approved natural alternatives
answer_text: Refrigerants with GWP greater than 700, including R-410A (GWP 2088) — new equipment must use lower-GWP alternatives like R-454B (GWP 466) or R-32 (GWP 675)
explanation: The AIM Act equipment ban under 40 CFR §84 prohibits the manufacture and importation of new high-pressure residential and light commercial air conditioning equipment using refrigerants with GWP greater than 700 after January 1, 2026. R-410A (GWP 2088) exceeds this limit and cannot be used in new equipment. R-454B (GWP 466) and R-32 (GWP 675) are both below 700 GWP and are approved for use in new equipment. Existing R-410A equipment installed before 2026 may continue operating and receiving service.
source_ref: 40 CFR §84; AIM Act HFC Phasedown Rule

question: Which of the following pieces of equipment MUST be spark-free (intrinsically safe) when used on systems containing A2L refrigerants such as R-454B or R-32?
options: Recovery machines, vacuum pumps, and manifold gauge sets — all equipment through which A2L refrigerant vapor may pass or that may be operated in the presence of A2L concentrations
Only the recovery machine — all other equipment is considered low-voltage and safe
Only brazing torches — electrical tools are exempt from the spark-free requirement
Spark-free equipment is not required for A2L refrigerants because they are not truly flammable
answer_text: Recovery machines, vacuum pumps, and manifold gauge sets — all equipment through which A2L refrigerant vapor may pass or that may be operated in the presence of A2L concentrations
explanation: A2L refrigerants are mildly flammable (flame speed ≤10 cm/s). Any equipment that handles A2L refrigerant vapor directly — including recovery machines (which compress refrigerant vapor), vacuum pumps (through which residual vapor passes during evacuation), and manifold gauge sets — should be rated or certified for A2L use with spark-free (intrinsically safe) internal components. Electronic leak detectors and refrigerant analyzers used near A2L systems should also be intrinsically safe. This precaution eliminates the risk of igniting refrigerant vapor at the equipment.
source_ref: ASHRAE Standard 15-2022; ASHRAE Standard 34-2022; EPA 608 Technician Certification Study Guide

question: A technician is servicing a new R-454B split system and plans to use a standard (non-spark-free) recovery machine. What risk does this create?
options: The electric motor and compressor contacts inside a non-spark-free recovery machine can produce sparks that could ignite R-454B vapor passing through the machine if the concentration exceeds the LFL
A non-spark-free recovery machine will not create sufficient vacuum to recover A2L refrigerant effectively
No risk — R-454B's LFL is so high that ignition inside a recovery machine is physically impossible
The risk is limited to the recovery cylinder, not the recovery machine itself
answer_text: The electric motor and compressor contacts inside a non-spark-free recovery machine can produce sparks that could ignite R-454B vapor passing through the machine if the concentration exceeds the LFL
explanation: A standard recovery machine contains an electric motor, compressor, relays, and internal contacts that can produce electrical sparks during operation. R-454B is A2L — mildly flammable. As R-454B vapor is drawn through the recovery machine during the recovery process, if the vapor concentration inside the machine reaches or exceeds the LFL, an internal spark from the motor or contacts could cause ignition. A2L-rated (spark-free) recovery machines are designed with explosion-proof or intrinsically safe electrical components that eliminate this internal ignition risk.

question: A walk-in freezer (commercial refrigeration) has a full charge of 75 lbs. The service log shows 18 lbs was added over the past year. What is the annual leak rate and is repair required under 40 CFR §82.157?
options: 24%; yes, it exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold — repair must begin within 30 days
24%; no, because the system is under 100 lbs and therefore exempt from the commercial refrigeration rule
18%; no, the leak rate does not exceed the 20% threshold
24%; yes, but only if the system also exceeds the 50 lb threshold (pre-2026) or 15 lb threshold (post-2026)
answer_text: 24%; yes, it exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold — repair must begin within 30 days
explanation: Annual leak rate = (18 ÷ 75) × 100 = 24%. Since this exceeds the 20% annual leak rate threshold for commercial refrigeration appliances under 40 CFR §82.157(b), a repair obligation is triggered. This 75 lb system exceeds both the pre-2026 (50 lb) and post-2026 (15 lb) charge thresholds, so it is fully subject to EPA leak repair requirements. The owner must repair the leak within 30 days of discovering the threshold exceedance per 40 CFR §82.157(d).

question: A technician proposes to save time by pressurizing a system with pure R-410A and immediately scanning all joints with a leak detector. Which statement best explains why this method violates EPA regulations even if all refrigerant is recovered afterward?
options: Pressurizing with pure refrigerant risks releasing refrigerant through the leak being tested — any refrigerant escaping during the test constitutes venting, which is prohibited under 40 CFR §82.154 regardless of whether remaining refrigerant is later recovered
Pure R-410A cannot be detected by standard halogen detectors, making the test ineffective
EPA requires all leak tests to use nitrogen, so any other gas — including refrigerant — is prohibited
The technician would need a special EPA permit to use pure refrigerant for leak testing purposes
answer_text: Pressurizing with pure refrigerant risks releasing refrigerant through the leak being tested — any refrigerant escaping during the test constitutes venting, which is prohibited under 40 CFR §82.154 regardless of whether remaining refrigerant is later recovered
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.154, knowingly venting refrigerant to the atmosphere is prohibited. When a system is pressurized with pure refrigerant and a leak test is performed, refrigerant escapes through any leak site present — this is precisely what the test is looking for. That escape constitutes venting. The fact that the technician recovers the bulk of the refrigerant afterward does not retroactively legalize the refrigerant that was released during testing. Using nitrogen/trace refrigerant or other non-destructive methods avoids this problem.

question: A pharmaceutical manufacturing plant has a large industrial process refrigeration system with a full charge of 800 lbs. The annual leak rate is 28%. Does this trigger a repair obligation under 40 CFR §82.157, and if so, what extended repair option may be available?
options: Yes, 28% does not exceed the 30% industrial process threshold — no repair obligation is triggered at this time
Yes, 28% exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold but not the 30% industrial process threshold — the system classification determines which applies
No, industrial process systems are exempt from all EPA leak repair requirements regardless of leak rate
Yes, 28% exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold — but since this is industrial process refrigeration, a 120-day extension (total) may be available if repair within 30 days is infeasible
answer_text: Yes, 28% does not exceed the 30% industrial process threshold — no repair obligation is triggered at this time
explanation: Industrial process refrigeration appliances under 40 CFR §82.157(b) are subject to a 30% annual leak rate threshold — the highest of the three categories. At a 28% annual leak rate, this system has not yet exceeded the 30% threshold, so no repair obligation is triggered at this time. The 28% rate is above the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold, but since this system is correctly classified as industrial process refrigeration (used in pharmaceutical manufacturing), the 30% threshold applies. The owner should monitor the system, as any further increase could push the rate above 30%.
